paragraphs.speaker: ben
paragraphs.name: Ben Gilbert
paragraphs.text: Welcome to season eight, episode seven of Acquired, the podcast about great technology companies and the stories and playbooks behind them. I'm Ben Gilbert and I am the co-founder and managing director of Seattle-based Pioneer Square Labs and our venture fund, PSL Ventures.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Rosenthal
paragraphs.text: And I'm David Rosenthal and I am an angel investor based in San Francisco. And we are your hosts. Well, David, here we are, the final episode in our Berkshire trilogy.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Rosenthal
paragraphs.text: Last we left Warren and Charlie in 1992, they, and in particular Warren, are heroes. Times have never been better. Warren is a legend. He literally single-handedly reversed a federal government decision and saved Salomon Brothers. His stature is unparalleled. He's the Oracle of Omaha. People are flocking to the annual shareholder meetings, Woodstock for Capitalists, attracting thousands of people filling arenas like a rock star.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Rosenthal
paragraphs.text: And Berkshire Stock passes ten thousand dollars a share, far and away the highest-priced single share of stock in history. Buffett himself is worth over five billion dollars. He's rocketing up the Forbes list, but there is one person out there in America and the world who is moving up that list faster than Warren, and fate is about to bring these two gentlemen together.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Rosenthal
paragraphs.text: Back in the previous summer of 1991, before Solomon, Kay Graham arranges for a Fourth of July weekend bash on Bainbridge Island in Seattle. She invites Warren along, of course. Part of the festivities planned is that on the fifth of July they're gonna go over to the Hood Canal and spend all day with a very prominent Seattle area family, Mary and Bill Gates Senior, and potentially their son, Bill Gates III, might drop by at some point during the day.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Rosenthal
paragraphs.text: Warren is a little reluctant to go on this trip. He's like, anything for Kay. Similarly, Bill Gates III does not have a lot of interest in going out on the fifth for this all-day event. He's super busy, he's running Microsoft, he wants to stay in Redmond and work. But Mary, his mom, forces him to come out. Bill would say later, I don't know about meeting a guy who just invests in money and picks stocks. I don't have many good questions for him.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Rosenthal
paragraphs.text: So Bill's plan is he's gonna fly in on the helicopter and then he's gonna make the helicopter wait there. He'll eat dinner and then escape, go back to work. So he's introduced to Warren and Warren immediately asks Bill what Bill thinks of IBM and whether they're gonna do well in the future. Gates is like, no, you should absolutely not buy IBM. You should buy two stocks and two stocks only, Microsoft and Intel, and you should just hold them. This is 1991. Microsoft at this point has about a ten billion dollar market cap and Intel has a three billion dollar market cap.

paragraphs.speaker: ben
paragraphs.name: Ben Gilbert
paragraphs.text: It is incredible that IBM, even though they made the computers, the value in the value chain did not accrue to them in any way, shape, or form. They became completely dumb terminals and all of the value was captured by the chip maker and by the operating system maker, which blindsided everyone.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Rosenthal
paragraphs.text: Gates turns around and asks Buffett about newspapers because Gates is probably already starting to think about coming after newspapers. Buffett is like, look, today newspapers are the best business out there. I'm thrilled to own them, but I gotta be honest with you, I am starting to worry about their future. I'm worried about the encroachment of TV and in particular cable television and people's news habits shifting to television, encroaching on newspapers.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Rosenthal
paragraphs.text: So they start talking and they sort of fall into conversation. At dinner, Bill Gates Senior asked this august group assembled there, what factor does everybody think has been most important in achieving where you've gotten in life? And Bill and Warren both immediately reply with one word, which is focus. So these guys are like, they are two peas in a pod. After dinner, the sun goes down, the helicopter leaves. Bill Gates, the third, stays. Warren has drawn him away from his work.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Rosenthal
paragraphs.text: So they become fast friends. On the first trading day after that, he makes another of his fateful, immediate split second gut stock purchases. He did buy Microsoft, just like he did with Geico. He bought one hundred shares of Microsoft for his personal account so that he could keep up with his friend, Bill Gates.

paragraphs.speaker: ben
paragraphs.name: Ben Gilbert
paragraphs.text: This decision is confounding to me.
